title: Random variables, expectation and variance
intro: So far we talked about probabilities of events. But in data we usually care about NUMBERS: how many items, how much money, how long. A random variable is exactly that — "a number that depends on chance". It has two main descriptors: the expectation (where its center is) and the variance (how widely it wanders around that center). These two concepts are the foundation everything that follows is built on.

beats.text: A random variable assigns a number to every random outcome. A die roll → the number shown; a user → how much they spent; a request → how many milliseconds the server took. Before you is a variable with five outcomes; bar height is the probability of each value. Drag the weights — the distribution changes.

The expectation E[X] is the average value of the variable if the experiment is repeated a great many times. It is computed simply: multiply each possible value by its probability and add up. For a die: E[X] = 1·⅙ + 2·⅙ + 3·⅙ + 4·⅙ + 5·⅙ + 6·⅙ = 3.5. It is the distribution's "center of gravity" (the green triangle under the chart is the balance point): not "the most frequent" and not even necessarily a possible value (a die never shows 3.5), but what the average over many repeats tends to — hello, law of large numbers.

The variance Var[X] = E[(X−E[X])²] is the average squared deviation from the expectation: how widely the variable is scattered. Its square root is σ, in original units. Push the weights to the edges — variance grows; pile them into one bar — it collapses to zero. These are exactly the "center and spread" from module one, only now for a random variable instead of a finished dataset.
Now the most important property. Press "show the sum of two independent X+X".
beats.predict: What happens to the expectation and variance of the sum of two independent identical variables — and to the shape?
beats.reveal: Both the expectation and the variance DOUBLE (for independent variables they add up), and the shape moves closer to a bell. That is the seed of two things at once: why the mean has spread σ/√n (add n variances, divide by n²) and why sums/averages tend to normal (the CLT).

beats.text: Remember the three bricks: a random variable (a number from chance), E[X] (the center), Var[X] (the spread) and the rule "variances of independent variables add". Everything downstream is built from them: the standard error, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, ANOVA and variance-reduction methods — it is all about expectations and variances of estimates.

definitions.text: a number that takes one value or another depending on the random outcome.
the variable's average over a long series of repeats: multiply each value xᵢ by its probability pᵢ and sum. It is the center of the distribution.
the average squared deviation from the expectation; σ = √Var is the spread in original units.
the variances of independent variables add up. The foundation of σ/√n, the CLT and ANOVA.
the outcome of one variable does not affect the distribution of the other. Without it, variance addition breaks.

practice: Expectation and variance are an analyst's working language. "How much will a user bring on average" is E[X]. "How unpredictable is revenue" is σ. Sample-size, confidence-interval and power calculations are the arithmetic of estimator variances.

The variance-addition rule explains why averaging calms noise: add n independent observations, divide by n — the mean's variance comes out n times smaller than a single observation's. Hence the σ/√n that will chase us through the whole course.
realLife: A stock portfolio: expected return is E[X], risk is σ. Diversification works precisely through variance addition: uncorrelated assets damp the total spread.
Reliability engineering and queueing: the expected number of events and its variance (Poisson) let you size capacity and price the risk of overload.
deepDive: Откуда берётся σ/√n — точность среднего. Сложим n независимых наблюдений: их дисперсии складываются, поэтому у суммы дисперсия равна n·σ². Среднее — это та же сумма, но делённая на n. А при делении величины на константу c её дисперсия делится на c² (разброс сжимается сильнее самого значения). Значит дисперсия среднего = n·σ² / n² = σ²/n, а стандартное отклонение среднего (его называют стандартной ошибкой) = σ/√n. Вот почему чем больше выборка, тем точнее среднее — и почему точность растёт не как n, а как √n.

Важная асимметрия двух правил. Математическое ожидание линейно ВСЕГДА: E[aX + b] = a·E[X] + b, и E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] даже для зависимых величин. А вот дисперсии складываются ТОЛЬКО у независимых величин. В общем случае Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2·Cov(X, Y), где Cov(X, Y) — ковариация, мера их совместного отклонения. Именно эту ковариацию используют методы снижения дисперсии: подобрав вспомогательную величину, тесно связанную с метрикой, из дисперсии оценки вычитают лишний разброс.
